# board.ps1 -- kanban ops by ISSUE NUMBER against GitHub Project 5 (#349 item 3).
#
# Wraps the manual `gh project` + GraphQL dance done by hand during the
# 2026-07-12 board passes (status moves, adds, top-of-column and after-item
# reordering). The project id, the Status field id, and its option ids are
# resolved at runtime every call via `gh project view` / `gh project
# field-list` -- never hardcoded, because they drifted once already.
#
# Pure parsing/lookup logic lives in Board.psm1 (Pester-tested with fixture
# JSON, no rig/token needed). This file is the thin gh wrapper: per the repo's
# TDD policy for CLI wrappers it is smoke-tested + documented rather than
# unit-tested (list + one reversible status round-trip on a closed Done issue;
# add/top/after are documented but not exercised against the live board -- see
# task-c2-report.md).
#
# Usage:
#   scripts/board.ps1 list [StatusName]
#   scripts/board.ps1 status <IssueNumber> <StatusName>
#   scripts/board.ps1 add <IssueNumber> [StatusName]
#   scripts/board.ps1 top <IssueNumber>
#   scripts/board.ps1 after <IssueNumber> <OtherIssueNumber>
#   scripts/board.ps1 status-batch <n1,n2,...> <StatusName>
#
# StatusName is validated against the LIVE field options (Backlog, Todo,
# In Progress, In Review, Done today, but never assumed).
#
# status-batch (#555): resolves project id + Status field + the item list
# ONCE, then applies N item-edits, instead of the per-call `status` command's
# full re-resolution each time (that pattern drained a whole GraphQL hour on
# ~20 operations on 2026-07-21). A single issue number missing from the board
# is reported and skipped, not fatal to the rest of the batch -- see
# Board.psm1's Resolve-BoardBatchItems (Pester-tested). An INDIVIDUAL gh
# item-edit failure mid-batch is likewise warned and skipped (the remaining
# edits still run; exit code 1 if anything missed or failed, 0 only on a
# fully clean batch) -- review 2026-07-21 asked for this to live here, not
# only in the commit message. Like the other write
# commands, this gh-wrapper loop is documented here rather than unit-tested
# (see task-c2-report.md precedent); only the parsing/lookup it calls into is
# unit-tested.
#
# Windows PowerShell 5.1 compatible: no ternary, no &&/||, ASCII only.

param(
    [Parameter(Mandatory, Position = 0)]
    [ValidateSet('list', 'status', 'add', 'top', 'after', 'status-batch')]
    [string]$Command,

    [Parameter(Position = 1)] [string]$Arg1,
    [Parameter(Position = 2)] [string]$Arg2,

    [string]$Owner = 'polecatspeaks',
    [int]$ProjectNumber = 6,
    [string]$Repo = 'polecatspeaks/StarCar'
)

Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'

Import-Module (Join-Path $PSScriptRoot 'Board.psm1') -Force

function Get-ProjectIdLive {
    $raw = gh project view $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project view $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
    ($raw | ConvertFrom-Json).id
}

function Get-StatusFieldLive {
    # --limit: gh defaults to 30 fields; same drift rationale as item-list's --limit 500 (review 2026-07-12)
    $raw = gh project field-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json --limit 100
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project field-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
    Get-BoardStatusField -FieldsResult ($raw | ConvertFrom-Json)
}

function Get-ItemsLive {
    # --limit 500: the project already has 200+ items (#349 ask: item-id
    # lookup must handle >200, well past gh's 30-item default).
    $raw = gh project item-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --format json --limit 500
    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { throw "gh project item-list $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)." }
    $raw | ConvertFrom-Json
}

function Get-RequiredItem {
    param([Parameter(Mandatory)] [object]$Items, [Parameter(Mandatory)] [int]$IssueNumber)

    $item = Find-BoardItemForIssue -ItemsResult $Items -IssueNumber $IssueNumber
    if (-not $item) {
        Write-Error "Issue #$IssueNumber is not on project $ProjectNumber. Use 'add' first."
        exit 1
    }
    return $item
}

switch ($Command) {
    'list' {
        $items = Get-ItemsLive
        $filterStatus = $Arg1
        foreach ($item in $items.items) {
            if ($filterStatus -and $item.status -ne $filterStatus) { continue }
            Write-Host (Format-BoardListLine -Item $item)
        }
        exit 0
    }

    'status' {
        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 status <IssueNumber> <StatusName>'
            exit 2
        }
        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
        $statusName = $Arg2

        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
        $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
        $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $statusName

        $items = Get-ItemsLive
        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber

        gh project item-edit --id $item.ItemId --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }

        Write-Host "#$issueNumber ($($item.Title)) -> $statusName"
        exit 0
    }

    'add' {
        if (-not $Arg1) {
            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 add <IssueNumber> [StatusName]'
            exit 2
        }
        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
        $url = "https://github.com/$Repo/issues/$issueNumber"

        $raw = gh project item-add $ProjectNumber --owner $Owner --url $url --format json
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-add failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
        $added = $raw | ConvertFrom-Json
        Write-Host "#$issueNumber added to project $ProjectNumber (item id $($added.id))."

        if ($Arg2) {
            $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
            $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
            $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $Arg2
            gh project item-edit --id $added.id --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }
            Write-Host "#$issueNumber -> $Arg2"
        }
        exit 0
    }

    'top' {
        if (-not $Arg1) {
            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 top <IssueNumber>'
            exit 2
        }
        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
        $items = Get-ItemsLive
        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber

        # Omitting afterId (not passing null/empty) is what moves the item to
        # the very top -- UpdateProjectV2ItemPositionInput.afterId is a
        # nullable, optional ID (confirmed via GraphQL schema introspection).
        $query = 'mutation($projectId: ID!, $itemId: ID!) { updateProjectV2ItemPosition(input: {projectId: $projectId, itemId: $itemId}) { clientMutationId } }'
        gh api graphql -f query=$query -f "projectId=$projectId" -f "itemId=$($item.ItemId)" | Out-Null
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh api graphql (updateProjectV2ItemPosition) failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }

        Write-Host "#$issueNumber moved to top."
        exit 0
    }

    'after' {
        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 after <IssueNumber> <OtherIssueNumber>'
            exit 2
        }
        $issueNumber = [int]$Arg1
        $otherIssueNumber = [int]$Arg2
        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
        $items = Get-ItemsLive
        $item = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $issueNumber
        $otherItem = Get-RequiredItem -Items $items -IssueNumber $otherIssueNumber

        $query = 'mutation($projectId: ID!, $itemId: ID!, $afterId: ID!) { updateProjectV2ItemPosition(input: {projectId: $projectId, itemId: $itemId, afterId: $afterId}) { clientMutationId } }'
        gh api graphql -f query=$query -f "projectId=$projectId" -f "itemId=$($item.ItemId)" -f "afterId=$($otherItem.ItemId)" | Out-Null
        if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error "gh api graphql (updateProjectV2ItemPosition) failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)."; exit $LASTEXITCODE }

        Write-Host "#$issueNumber moved after #$otherIssueNumber."
        exit 0
    }

    'status-batch' {
        if (-not $Arg1 -or -not $Arg2) {
            Write-Error 'Usage: board.ps1 status-batch <n1,n2,...> <StatusName>'
            exit 2
        }
        $statusName = $Arg2
        $issueNumbers = ConvertFrom-BoardIssueNumberList -IssueNumbersText $Arg1

        # Resolve metadata ONCE (#555): this is the whole point of the batch
        # command over N `status` calls, each of which re-resolves all three.
        $projectId = Get-ProjectIdLive
        $statusField = Get-StatusFieldLive
        $optionId = Resolve-BoardStatusOptionId -StatusField $statusField -StatusName $statusName
        $items = Get-ItemsLive

        $resolution = Resolve-BoardBatchItems -ItemsResult $items -IssueNumbers $issueNumbers

        foreach ($missingIssue in $resolution.Missing) {
            Write-Warning "#$missingIssue is not on project $ProjectNumber. Skipped (use 'add' first)."
        }

        $editFailures = @()
        foreach ($found in $resolution.Found) {
            gh project item-edit --id $found.ItemId --field-id $statusField.FieldId --project-id $projectId --single-select-option-id $optionId | Out-Null
            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) {
                Write-Warning "#$($found.IssueNumber) ($($found.Title)): gh project item-edit failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE). Skipped, continuing batch."
                $editFailures += $found.IssueNumber
                continue
            }
            Write-Host "#$($found.IssueNumber) ($($found.Title)) -> $statusName"
        }

        $editedCount = $resolution.Found.Count - $editFailures.Count
        Write-Host "status-batch: $editedCount edited, $($resolution.Missing.Count) missing, $($editFailures.Count) edit failure(s)."

        if ($resolution.Missing.Count -gt 0 -or $editFailures.Count -gt 0) { exit 1 }
        exit 0
    }
}

#!/bin/sh
# SessionStart hook: announce a /goodnight resume packet if one exists.
# Written by the goodnight skill (.claude/skills/goodnight/SKILL.md) when a session
# closes with agents still in flight. Background agents die with their session, so the
# packet is a re-dispatch spec the next session must read FIRST.
packet="$HOME/.claude/projects/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/memory/resume-packet.md"
if [ -f "$packet" ]; then
  echo "[goodnight] RESUME PACKET FOUND from the previous session: $packet"
  echo "[goodnight] Unfinished work is recorded there. Read it FIRST, fold it into the"
  echo "[goodnight] working-state memory, re-dispatch what it names, then DELETE the packet."
fi
exit 0

content.content: Launching skill: superpowers:brainstorming

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: C:\Users\Chris\.claude\plugins\cache\claude-plugins-official\superpowers\6.1.1\skills\brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer the visual companion just-in-time** — NOT upfront. The first time a question would genuinely be clearer shown than described, offer it then (its own message); on approval its browser tab opens for you. If no visual question ever arises, never offer it. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion (just-in-time):** Do NOT offer it upfront. Wait until a question would genuinely be clearer shown than told — a real mockup / layout / diagram question, not merely a UI *topic*. The first time that happens, offer it then, as its own message:
> "This next part might be easier if I show you — I can put together mockups, diagrams, and comparisons in a browser tab as we go. It's still new and can be token-intensive. Want me to? I'll open it for you."

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Only the offer — no clarifying question, summary, or other content. Wait for the user's response. If they accept, start the server with `--open` so their browser opens to the first screen automatically. If they decline, continue text-only and don't offer again unless they raise it.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

lastPrompt: Hello buddy, welcome to the StarCar Repo. It's a fresh repo for us to work out of. Two initial taskings: Read all the documentation and once you've ingested that and executed any tasking required by i…

aiTitle: Review StarCar repo documentation and first issue

content.text:
Docs ingested — all of it: `README.md`, `docs/constitution.md` (8 laws, ratified today), `docs/the-healing-loop.md`, `CLAUDE.md` (the process seed + the HARD INVARIANT), `docs/setup.md`, the three templates, `.claude/agents/car.md`, the `/goodnight` skill, `.mcp.json`, `.claude/settings.json`, and the board scripts.

**Tasking executed from the docs:**
- SessionStart resume-packet hook fired and announced nothing → no work parked from a prior session. Clean start.
- `docs/setup.md` trigger-gated installs: GitNexus index (trigger = *first code lands*), CI (trigger = *first workflow need*), session-start env hook (trigger = *web/container env*), suite runner (trigger = *first test suite*). **None of those triggers have fired.** Right-sizing rule says install when the trigger fires, not before — so I installed nothing.
- HARD INVARIANT acknowledged: this session is StarCar-only. Nothing from any other project gets typed here.

**Two findings from the read (both small, both reportable):**

1. **`scripts/board.ps1:1` lies about what it targets.** The header comment says *"against GitHub Project 5 (#349 item 3)"* and cites `task-c2-report.md` and issues #349/#555 — all ancestor-shop artifacts that don't exist in this repo. The actual defaults (`board.ps1:52-53`) are project **6**, `polecatspeaks/StarCar`. Constitution Law 1 turned on our own tooling: a comment describing a different project is exactly the lying-canary disease. Cheap fix, but it should be a ticket, not a silent edit.

2. **Issue #1 has no label, and no area taxonomy exists.** `CLAUDE.md` Tracking says *one area label per issue* and *a train is composed from ONE label's tickets*. The repo carries only GitHub's stock labels (bug/enhancement/documentation/…) — there is no area vocabulary yet. With one issue it costs nothing; at ten issues it costs a manifest.

---

**On issue #1 itself:** it's the founding epic, not a task — `The yard visualizer: tracks, signals, freight, and fuel`, Todo on project 6, sole item on the board. It already carries a design sketch from the founding-day brainstorm (four surfaces, pluggable adapters, Laws 5/6/7 cited) and names the route: *v0 static mock first to validate the layout, then the adapter/generator through the full ladder*.

So the right next move is to turn that sketch into a reviewable design doc — which is the ladder's first rung. I'm using the brainstorming skill to do that, and I need a handful of decisions from you first. One at a time.

Starting with the one that constrains everything downstream:

questions.question: What is StarCar, as a thing that runs? The founding sketch fixes the content (trains/gates/freight/fuel) but not the medium, and the medium constrains the mock, the adapters, the test strategy, and what "a stranger can deploy this" (Law 7) even means.

options.label: Static-generating web board (Recommended)
options.description: A generator reads adapters and emits a self-contained HTML page; you open it in a browser, refresh to re-poll. No server, no runtime deps for the viewer. Cheapest v0 (the mock IS the target format, so the mock is not throwaway), trivially publishable as the public showcase, and a stranger deploys it by running one command. Weakest at live/push updates.
options.preview:
generator (adapters -> state.json) --> index.html
                                       ^
                                       | open in browser, F5 to refresh

- v0 mock = hand-written index.html, same shape
- no server, no viewer deps
- publishable straight to GH Pages

options.label: Local web app w/ live server
options.description: A small server process polls adapters and pushes updates to a browser (SSE/websocket). True live wall-board on a second monitor. Costs a running process, a port, a lifecycle, and reconnect/staleness handling as first-class work from day one (Laws 4 and 5 get expensive early).
options.preview:
adapters -> server (poll loop) --SSE--> browser
                |
                +-- /health, staleness, reconnect

- v0 mock = static HTML, later thrown away or wrapped
- live updates, no refresh
- process lifecycle is real work

options.label: Terminal TUI
options.description: Runs in the terminal beside the agent sessions it watches; no browser, no port. Fits the dispatcher-at-a-console feel and is fast to iterate. But a rail-yard board is a spatial, graphical thing, and the public showcase becomes a screenshot/asciicast rather than a link anyone can click.
options.preview:
+- TRAINS ------------------+- GATES -+
| T1 |car1 OK|car2 ~~|      | D S P C |
| T2 |car3 XX|             | G G . . |
+---------------------------+---------+

- no browser, no port
- showcase = asciicast, not a URL

The founding sketch fixes the content (trains/gates/freight/fuel) but not the medium, and the medium constrains the mock, the adapters, the test strategy, and what "a stranger can deploy this" (Law 7) even means."="Local web app w/ live server" selected preview:

                |
                +-- /health, staleness, reconnect

- v0 mock = static HTML, later thrown away or wrapped
- live updates, no refresh
- process lifecycle is real work. You can now continue with these answers in mind.

answers.What is StarCar, as a thing that runs? T: Local web app w/ live server

What is StarCar, as a thing that runs? T.preview:
adapters -> server (poll loop) --SSE--> browser
                |
                +-- /health, staleness, reconnect

- v0 mock = static HTML, later thrown away or wrapped
- live updates, no refresh
- process lifecycle is real work

content.text:
Locked: **live server + browser**, which makes process lifecycle, staleness, and reconnect first-class design work rather than something deferred — Laws 4 and 5 get paid for up front. Noted that the v0 static mock is then a throwaway layout probe, not the shipping artifact; that's a real cost and I'll size it accordingly in the design.

Next decision:

questions.question: What stack does the server run on? This picks the test runner, the CI workflow, and what "a stranger deploys this" costs them (Law 7). It also decides whether the browser side shares a language with the server.

options.label: TypeScript on Node (Recommended)
options.description: One language server-and-browser, so adapter types are shared with the view rather than mirrored (kills a whole sentence-check failure class before it exists). SSE is built in, vitest gives fast red-first cycles, and a stranger needs only Node. Cost: npm dependency hygiene, and TS build config is its own small tax.
options.preview:
server: node + express/hono, res.write() SSE
view:   plain TS + DOM (no framework at v0)
test:   vitest, red-first, fast watch
ship:   npm i && npm start  (stranger: 1 dep, Node)

shared types = adapters and view cannot drift

options.label: .NET / C#
options.description: Native fit for a Windows box, strong typing, first-class background-service lifecycle (exactly the staleness/reconnect surface this design has to nail), and xUnit is mature. Cost: the browser side is a separate language, so DTOs become hand-maintained mirrors - the exact class the sentence check exists to catch. Stranger needs the SDK.
options.preview:
server: ASP.NET minimal API + BackgroundService
view:   JS/TS in wwwroot (separate language)
test:   xUnit
ship:   dotnet run  (stranger: .NET SDK)

DTO <-> view = hand-maintained mirror (scar zone)

options.label: Python
options.description: Fastest to sketch, excellent for the git/GitHub adapters (rich client libraries), pytest is superb for red-first. Cost: same two-language split as .NET, async SSE story is workable but fiddlier, and the stranger deploys into Python-environment roulette (venv, versions).
options.preview:
server: FastAPI + EventSourceResponse
view:   JS in templates (separate language)
test:   pytest
ship:   uv run  (stranger: venv/version care)

best adapter libs, weakest deploy story

content.text:
Good — that kills the DTO-mirror failure class by construction rather than by review, which is the Healing Loop's step-2 preference (structural guard beats a review rule).

Now the crux of the whole project, and the one I can't infer from the docs:

questions.question: Where does train/car/gate truth actually come from? Law 6 says adapters own the facts and the view never derives a verdict the source did not issue - so whichever source is first, its authority has to be real, not inferred. Which adapter is the first real one?

options.label: Conductor state file (Recommended)
options.description: A JSON file the conductor (me, during a train) writes as work moves: trains, cars, gate verdicts, SHAs. It is the only source that actually KNOWS a REJECT happened - nothing else in the system records one. Zero inference means zero Law 1 risk, and the file doubles as the in-repo synthetic demo data Law 7 asks for. Cost: it is bookkeeping, and a conductor who forgets to write makes a stale board.
options.preview:
state.json (conductor-written, git-tracked)
  trains[] -> cars[] -> {state, sha, reviewer, verdict}
  gates[]  -> {name, aspect, at}

+ knows REJECTs (nothing else does)
+ zero inference -> zero Law 1 risk
+ IS the demo fixture, in-repo
-  staleness depends on conductor discipline

options.label: Git adapter (branches/worktrees/commits)
options.description: Infers train and car state from repo reality - worktrees present, branches ahead/behind, commit SHAs, merge status. No bookkeeping burden and it cannot go stale. But git records no gate verdicts at all, so the signals surface stays empty or gets INFERRED, which is the Law 1/Law 6 violation the constitution names most explicitly.
options.preview:
git -> worktrees, branches, SHAs, merged?

+ zero bookkeeping, never stale
+ car coupling state is genuinely knowable
-  NO verdicts exist in git
-  inferring them = Law 6 violation by design

options.label: GitHub board adapter (project 6)
options.description: Reads issues, labels, and Status columns straight from project 6 via the API. Real data exists right now (the board scripts already resolve it), and it maps cleanly onto the inbound-freight surface. But it renders the QUEUE, not the trains - it says nothing about cars in flight, gates, or verdicts, so the marquee surface stays mocked.

+ real data TODAY, adapter logic mostly exists
+ inbound freight surface becomes true
-  says nothing about trains/cars/gates
-  marquee surface stays fake

content.text:
Locked. That also means the state file is simultaneously the adapter's source, the in-repo demo fixture (Law 7), and the test corpus — one artifact doing three jobs, which is cheap in the right way.

One design observation before the next question, because it changes the options: **Law 4 says a missing lane reading as "no trains" is a lie of omission.** So any surface without an adapter must render as *"no adapter connected"*, loudly — not as empty. That makes showing all four lanes early nearly free and constitutionally *required* rather than optional, which changes what the first train should look like:

You see the real layout, in the real browser, over the real SSE pipe, with nothing thrown away. Supersedes the static mock: the honest empty-state IS the mock, and it ships.

content.text:
Four decisions locked. That supersedes issue #1's "static mock first" route, so **the issue needs an amendment comment recording the change and why** (the docs' own rule: rulings get recorded, not silently applied). I'll do that once you approve the design.

Here's the design.

---

## StarCar v0 — the walking skeleton

### Architecture (six units, each independently testable)

**1. `Adapter<T>` — the contract.** One method, `poll(): Promise<AdapterResult<T>>`, which returns *either* data with an `asOf` stamp *or* an explicit failure with a human-readable reason. **It never throws into the view and health is part of the return value, not a side channel.** Law 5 makes adapter health a first-class surface; putting it in the return type means a lane physically cannot render without carrying its own health.

**2. `StateFileAdapter`** — reads `state.json`, validates it against a schema, returns a `TrainsSnapshot` or an `AdapterFailure` naming the validation error.

**3. `SnapshotAssembler`** — combines adapter results into a `YardSnapshot`. This is where Law 4 stops being prose and becomes a type:

```ts
type LaneState<T> =
  | { kind: 'ok';         data: T; asOf: string }
  | { kind: 'stale';      data: T; asOf: string; ageMs: number }
  | { kind: 'failed';     reason: string; lastGood?: T }
  | { kind: 'no-adapter' }

type YardSnapshot = {
  asOf: string
  lanes: { trains: LaneState<Train[]>; signals: LaneState<Gate[]>;
           freight: LaneState<Ticket[]>; fuel: LaneState<Fuel> }
}
```

`lanes` has four **required** keys and every value is a discriminated union. There is no way to express "this lane is absent" — the compiler will not let you omit a lane or hand back a bare array. *A lie of omission becomes a type error.* That's the Healing Loop's step-2 preference: a structural impossibility beats a review rule.

**4. `Server`** — serves the view assets, `GET /api/snapshot` (first paint), `GET /api/stream` (SSE). Poll loop on an interval; pushes only when the snapshot changes.

**5. `View`** — plain TS + DOM, no framework. Pure `render(snapshot) → DOM`, so it tests under jsdom without a browser.

**6. `Staleness`** — deliberately *two* independent truths, never merged: the **server** knows "my last successful poll was N seconds ago" (data stale); the **client** knows "my SSE pipe is down" (connection lost). These fail independently and a board that collapses them into one indicator will lie in exactly one of the two cases. Law 6: where two sources disagree, show the disagreement.

### Data flow

```
state.json --poll--> StateFileAdapter --> SnapshotAssembler --> /api/snapshot ---> View --> DOM
                                                            \-> /api/stream (SSE) -/
```

### Error handling

| Failure | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Adapter throws | Caught at boundary → `{kind:'failed', reason}` → red panel showing the reason. Never a blank lane. |
| Malformed `state.json` | Validation error → `failed` lane with the error text. **No partial render** (Law 1: never render what you can't back). |
| File missing / unreadable | `failed` with the OS reason. |
| SSE disconnect | Client shows "disconnected, last good as-of X", reconnects with backoff. Data stays on screen but *visibly* marked. |
| Lane has no adapter yet | `no-adapter` panel, loud. |

### Testing (all red-first)

Fixture corpus of `state.json` variants (valid / malformed / missing / partial) drives the adapter tests. The load-bearing ones: **a test asserting an unimplemented lane renders `no-adapter` and not empty** (Law 4 pinned mechanically), and **a fake-clock staleness test** crossing the threshold.

This train lands the first mutable service state — `lastGoodSnapshot`, `lastPollAt`, `connectedClients` — so **`docs/contracts/state-ledger.md` gets instantiated in the same commit**, with StarCar's lifecycle events enumerated for the first time: *server restart · adapter failure window · client reconnect · state-file replaced-or-truncated*. Each field gets a red-first verdict against all four.

### Explicitly NOT in this train (YAGNI)

No git adapter. No board adapter. No fuel adapter. No auth. No persistence. No framework. No CLI for writing `state.json` (hand-written and git-tracked at v0). No theming beyond one legible look.

### Cost line (CLAUDE.md cost discipline — approve before dispatch)

Full ladder is ~11 dispatches: design review 1, spec 1 + review 1, plan 1 + review 1, then ~3 cars each with its own reviewer. A right-sized alternative folds design and spec into one document (at this size the design *is* the spec), saving 2.

---

questions.question: Does this design hold, and how much ladder does it get? Cost discipline says the budget owner approves spend along with scope, before dispatch.

options.label: Approved - fold design+spec (Recommended)
options.description: Design holds. Write it as one design/spec document, send it to ONE adversarial design review, then plan -> plan review -> cars. ~9 dispatches. Justification: the design already fixes the data contract to type-level precision, so a separate spec document would mostly restate it - and CLAUDE.md warns that maximum ceremony on every change is itself a process failure. The design review still holds full REJECT authority.
options.preview:
design/spec doc  -> adversarial review   (2)
plan             -> adversarial review   (2)
car A adapter+assembler -> reviewer      (2)
car B server+SSE        -> reviewer      (2)
car C view+staleness    -> reviewer      (2)
                                    ~9-10 total

options.label: Approved - full ladder, no folding
options.description: Design holds, and it gets the complete treatment: separate design doc, design review, separate spec, spec review, plan, plan review, then cars. ~11 dispatches. Buys the extra gate on the founding subsystem specifically because it is the founding subsystem - every later train inherits these contracts as ground truth, and a flawed spec is the most expensive document in the pipeline.

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
gh issue create --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --label "area:tooling" \
  --title "board.ps1 header comment describes the ancestor project, not StarCar" \
  --body "\`scripts/board.ps1\` opens with a header block ported verbatim from the shop it came from. It says the script operates \"against GitHub Project 5 (#349 item 3)\" and cites \`task-c2-report.md\`, issue #349, and issue #555 - none of which exist in this repo. The real defaults are at board.ps1:52-53: project **6**, repo \`polecatspeaks/StarCar\`.

Constitution Law 1 (Truth) applied to our own tooling: a comment that confidently describes a different project is the lying-canary pattern the Healing Loop names - the reader trusts the documented mechanism while the code does something else. Same class as the gating-matrix template's stated rationale, just aimed inward.

Scope: rewrite the header to describe StarCar's board, keep the design rationale that still applies (runtime id resolution because ids drifted; --limit flags; the batch command's one-resolution rationale; PS 5.1 constraints), drop or re-anchor every reference to an artifact that does not exist here.

Coverage-class defect, not structural - EXTEND with a small rider, one car and one reviewer."
echo "---"
gh issue create --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --label "area:process" \
  --title "No area-label taxonomy existed; issue #1 was unlabeled" \
  --body "CLAUDE.md's Tracking rule requires one area label per issue and composes a train from ONE label's tickets (single coherent manifest). The repo carried only GitHub's stock label set, so that rule was unenforceable from day one and issue #1 shipped unlabeled.

Filed for the record rather than fixed silently - 'no untracked work' applies to process repairs too, and the Healing Loop's step 4 is that the fix gets written into the institution rather than just done.

Resolution (owner-approved, founding day): five area labels created - \`area:view\`, \`area:adapters\`, \`area:server\`, \`area:process\`, \`area:tooling\`. Issue #1 labeled \`area:view\`.

Remaining, deliberately NOT done here: nothing enforces the rule mechanically. A prose rule binds an agent that reads it; only a structural guard binds every agent. A label-presence check belongs in the CI workflow whenever CI lands (docs/setup.md gates that on 'first workflow need'). Noting it here so the gap is written down rather than assumed closed."

content.text:
Board synced — and it surfaced a third finding immediately.

**Finding 3: project 6 can't represent the process it feeds.** Its Status field offers only `Todo / In Progress / Done`. The ladder needs **In Review** as a real state (a car sitting at inspection awaiting its adversarial reviewer is neither in progress nor done — and "a car at inspection looks in-progress" is precisely the Law 1 falsehood StarCar exists to kill). Issue #1's sketch also names a **holding yard** for deferred work, which wants Backlog. The board is a planned adapter source, so its columns being narrower than the process is a data-truth problem, not a cosmetic one. `board.ps1`'s live-option validation is what caught it — the script refusing to accept `Backlog` rather than guessing is the tooling behaving exactly as designed.

Filed so far: **#2** (`area:tooling`), **#3** (`area:process`), both Todo on the board. #1 now labeled `area:view`.

Now, back to the design — I need to know what to fix:

content.text:
Scope revision taken: **the writer is in, the board adapter stays out, nothing gets cut.** Here's the delta — I'm not re-presenting the parts you didn't object to.

---

## Revision: `StateWriter` joins as unit 7

**What it is.** A typed module plus a thin CLI wrapper (`starcar car set c2 rolling --sha abc1234`, `starcar gate set plan-review reject`). The module holds all logic and gets unit-tested; the CLI shell is smoke-tested and documented. That split isn't invented — it's the precedent `board.ps1:29-39` already set in this repo for gh wrappers, so the two pieces of tooling will follow one policy instead of two.

**Three properties, each load-bearing:**

**1. Writer and reader import the same schema module.** If the writer had its own notion of the shape, we would have rebuilt the hand-maintained mirror the TypeScript decision was chosen to eliminate — writing the exact scar back into the design while congratulating ourselves for avoiding it. One schema, two importers, compiler-enforced.

**2. Writes are atomic: temp file, then rename.** Without this the poller eventually reads a half-flushed file, fails validation, and paints *"malformed state"* on a board whose state was perfectly fine — a **confident falsehood on a status surface**, which Law 1 names as the worst defect this project can ship. It would also be intermittent and blame the wrong component. Atomic rename makes the reader see only whole files, always.

**3. Validation on write does NOT retire validation on read.** `state.json` is git-tracked and hand-editable; a human, a merge conflict, or a bad rebase can still produce garbage the writer never saw. Both sides validate. Belt and braces, because the two failure paths are genuinely different.

**Consequence that changes an earlier decision: the server polls by mtime, it does not `fs.watch`.** Atomic rename swaps the inode, and `fs.watch` handles that poorly (missed events, or events on a stale handle — worse on Windows). Polling is the dumber mechanism and the correct one. The design said "poll loop" before as a default; now it's a decision with a reason attached, which is the difference between a choice and an accident.

**Ledger impact:** the writer is a one-shot process and holds no long-lived state, so it adds no ledger rows. But it *creates* the `state-file replaced-or-truncated` lifecycle event that was previously hypothetical — the ledger's fourth column now has a real mechanism behind it, and `lastGoodSnapshot` has to be verdicted against an inode swap mid-poll.

**Still explicitly cut:** git adapter, GitHub board adapter, fuel adapter, auth, persistence, framework, theming, and any history log (`state.json` is a current-state snapshot; a REJECT round is a counter, not an event stream).

**Revised cars:** A — schema + adapter + assembler · B — writer + CLI · C — server + SSE + poll + ledger · D — view + staleness + honest-empty panels.

---

One design/spec document, ONE adversarial design review holding full REJECT authority, then plan -> plan review -> four cars each with its own reviewer. ~12 dispatches. The data contract is already specified to type-level precision, so a separate spec would largely restate it, and CLAUDE.md warns that maximum ceremony on every change is itself a process failure.

content.text:
Of course — what would you like to clarify?

To make that easier, here's what's actually on the table in that question, in case the ambiguity is mine:

- **The design itself** — whether the seven units, the `LaneState` contract, the writer's shape, and the atomic-write/poll-not-watch calls are right.
- **The ladder shape** — whether design and spec collapse into one document or stay as two gates. This is purely a question of how many adversarial gates the founding contracts pass through before code gets written.
- **The dispatch count** — ~12 vs ~14 agent dispatches, which is the spend you'd be approving. I quoted it as a range because a REJECT at any gate adds a round, and REJECTs are expected outcomes here, not failures.

Anything else you want to poke at first — the cost basis, what a "car" actually costs in practice, whether four cars is the right split of that work, whether the state file schema should be nailed down before any of this, or something I haven't raised at all — ask and I'll answer straight rather than steering you back to the menu.

file.content:
- Reviewers run the suites themselves and re-verify claims empirically - a review that
  only reads the report is a spelling check.
- A REJECT is a success outcome for the process. Scorecards count catches, not friction.

*Scar: the day this calibration was ratified, reviewers rejected their own train's plan
twice and a merged-that-day instrument was found crying wolf on 50 of 54 flags. All three
catches were cheaper than any of the incidents they prevented.*

## The sentence check (cross-boundary tracing)

When a diff produces, threads, or consumes a value that crosses a process or serialization
boundary, the reviewer MUST trace the full production path - every hop named with
file:line, every hand-maintained mirror (DTO, wire sample, snapshot) checked for the field
- and state the trace in the verdict. "Each file is correct" is a spelling check; the
review is not complete until someone has read the whole sentence.

*Scar: nine wire fields once crossed sixteen correct, reviewed files - and a third process
hop outside every review's scope silently dropped all nine. Every test passed because each
read one page of a three-page sentence. The first end-to-end reader was production. Paid
twice (a max-laps field died the same way earlier) before becoming law.*

## Dispatch rules (multi-agent hygiene)

- **Name the model explicitly on every dispatch.** Never let a subagent default. *Scar: an
  unnamed model once stalled a whole evening's pipeline.*
- **No nested delegation - structurally.** Subagents never spawn subagents; the conductor
  is the only fan-out level. Enforce it by TOOLSET (the implementer agent type simply has
  no dispatch tool), not by prose. *Scar: a reviewer once forked itself mid-review; the
  owner had to stop the train by hand. A prose rule binds an agent that reads it; a
  toolset binds every agent regardless.*

input.old_string: ## Dispatch rules (multi-agent hygiene)
input.new_string:
## Branch topology and landing (STANDING ORDER)

**Never commit to `main`.** Nothing reaches `main` except by pull request. `main` is the
published face of a repo whose whole thesis is that the process is visible and real, so
its history is a claim about how the work was done, and a direct commit is that claim
being made falsely.

**Documentation lands the same way code does, through the same gates.** In this project
the documents ARE the product as much as the code is: designs, specs, plans, review
verdicts, ledgers, and the operating rules themselves. A doc that skips review because
"it is only prose" is the highest-leverage unreviewed artifact in the pipeline - plans,
cars, and reviewers all consume it as ground truth. Prose gets no discount.

- Work integrates on `dev`. Car branches cut from `dev`, merge back to `dev`.
- `dev` reaches `main` by PR, reviewed, never by fast-forward push.
- The rule is enforced by branch protection on the remote, not by memory. A prose rule
  binds whoever reads it; a protected branch binds every actor, including a future agent
  that never read this file.

*Scar: on founding day the repo's first five commits went straight to `main` because no
one had said not to yet, and the session was one approval away from committing the
founding design document the same way - the document every later contract would have
inherited as ground truth. Caught by the owner, not by a gate. The order was issued
before the design landed, which is the only reason it cost nothing.*

## Dispatch rules (multi-agent hygiene)

## Branch topology and landing (STANDING ORDER)

**Never commit to `main`.** Nothing reaches `main` except by pull request. `main` is the
published face of a repo whose whole thesis is that the process is visible and real, so
its history is a claim about how the work was done, and a direct commit is that claim
being made falsely.

**Documentation lands the same way code does, through the same gates.** In this project
the documents ARE the product as much as the code is: designs, specs, plans, review
verdicts, ledgers, and the operating rules themselves. A doc that skips review because
"it is only prose" is the highest-leverage unreviewed artifact in the pipeline - plans,
cars, and reviewers all consume it as ground truth. Prose gets no discount.

- Work integrates on `dev`. Car branches cut from `dev`, merge back to `dev`.
- `dev` reaches `main` by PR, reviewed, never by fast-forward push.
- The rule is enforced by branch protection on the remote, not by memory. A prose rule
  binds whoever reads it; a protected branch binds every actor, including a future agent
  that never read this file.

*Scar: on founding day the repo's first five commits went straight to `main` because no
one had said not to yet, and the session was one approval away from committing the
founding design document the same way - the document every later contract would have
inherited as ground truth. Caught by the owner, not by a gate. The order was issued
before the design landed, which is the only reason it cost nothing.*

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
gh issue create --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --label "area:process" \
  --title "STANDING ORDER: never commit to main; everything lands by PR, docs included" \
  --body "Owner standing order, issued 2026-07-21 before the founding design document was written.

**The order:** never commit to \`main\`. Code reaches \`main\` by pull request only. And documentation is as critical as the code - it lands through the same gates, with no discount for being prose.

**Why it matters here specifically:** this repo's thesis is that the process is visible and real, so \`main\`'s history is itself a claim about how the work was done. A direct commit makes that claim falsely. And in a project whose deliverables are largely documents (designs, specs, plans, verdicts, ledgers), exempting prose from review would exempt most of the product.

**Done:**
- \`dev\` branch created and pushed; work integrates there, car branches cut from and merge back to \`dev\`.
- Rule written into \`CLAUDE.md\` as a STANDING ORDER section with its scar attached (Healing Loop step 4: the written system is the only system that survives the night).

**Open:**
- Branch protection on \`main\` - the mechanical guard (Healing Loop step 2: a structural guard beats a prose rule, because a prose rule binds only whoever reads it).
- \`docs/setup.md\` lists 'CI + repo policy' as trigger-gated on 'first workflow need'. That trigger has now partially fired: repo policy is needed today, CI still is not. Update the row so the doc does not describe a state we have left.
- Nothing yet enforces that a PR touching \`docs/\` got a review. That is a CI-time check and stays parked until CI lands."
echo "--- issue filed ---"
git add CLAUDE.md
git commit -q -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
docs: STANDING ORDER - never commit to main, everything lands by PR, docs included

Owner order issued on founding day, one approval before the first design
document would have gone straight to main the same way the first five
commits did. Recorded with its scar attached, per the Healing Loop's step 4:
a rule that lives only in a session log is gone by morning.

Documentation gets no discount for being prose. In this project the
documents are the product as much as the code - and a spec that skips
review is the most expensive unreviewed artifact in the pipeline, because
plans, cars, and reviewers all consume it as ground truth.

Branch protection (the mechanical guard, Healing Loop step 2) is the open
half of this and is proposed to the owner separately - a prose rule binds
whoever reads it, a protected branch binds every actor.

Refs #4

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

questions.question: How should branch protection on main be configured? GitHub does not permit approving your own pull request, so on a solo-owner repo "require 1 approval" does not mean careful - it means every PR is unmergeable until a second human account approves it.

options.label: Require PR, 0 approvals, admins exempt (Recommended)
options.description: Forces every change through a PR and blocks force-push and deletion on main, but requires no approval click and leaves you able to override in an emergency. Binds agents absolutely - no agent can push to main by any path - while honoring Law 2, the dispatcher outranks the board and the system never resists an override. The real review gate is the adversarial reviewer, not GitHub's approve button.
options.preview:
require pull request      : YES
required approvals        : 0
block force push          : YES
block branch deletion     : YES
enforce for admins        : NO  (you can override)

agents: cannot reach main, period
owner : can override deliberately

options.label: Require PR, 0 approvals, admins INCLUDED
options.description: Same, but the rule binds you too - no override path without first going into settings and turning protection off, which makes any bypass a deliberate, visible act rather than a reflex. Stronger guarantee that main's history is an honest record. Cost: a 3am fix to a broken main needs a settings round-trip, and it sits in tension with Law 2.
options.preview:
require pull request      : YES
required approvals        : 0
block force push          : YES
block branch deletion     : YES
enforce for admins        : YES (no quiet bypass)

bypass = visible, deliberate settings change
tension with Law 2 (dispatcher commands)

options.label: Require PR AND 1 approval
options.description: The strictest reading of the order. Be aware of the consequence: you cannot approve your own PR on GitHub, so unless a second account or a bot reviews, main becomes effectively frozen. Choose this only if you intend to add a reviewing account - otherwise it enforces the order by making landing impossible.
options.preview:
require pull request      : YES
required approvals        : 1

!! you cannot approve your own PR
!! main is unmergeable without a 2nd account

only viable if a reviewer account is coming

options.label: Hold off, prose rule for now
options.description: Leave the remote unprotected today and rely on the CLAUDE.md rule plus my compliance. Faster, reversible, no settings surface changed. Cost: this is precisely the gap the Healing Loop names - the distance between what is written and what is enforced - and the standing order was issued because prose already failed once today.
options.preview:
written rule : YES (CLAUDE.md)
enforced     : NO

binds: whoever reads CLAUDE.md
binds not: any agent that does not

revisit when CI lands

content.text: Applying exactly what was approved — nothing extra:

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
gh api -X PUT repos/polecatspeaks/StarCar/branches/main/protection --input - <<'EOF' > /dev/null
{
  "required_status_checks": null,
  "enforce_admins": false,
  "required_pull_request_reviews": {
    "required_approving_review_count": 0,
    "dismiss_stale_reviews": false,
    "require_code_owner_reviews": false
  },
  "restrictions": null,
  "allow_force_pushes": false,
  "allow_deletions": false
}
EOF
echo "--- applied, reading back live config ---"
gh api repos/polecatspeaks/StarCar/branches/main/protection \
  --jq '{require_pr: (.required_pull_request_reviews != null), approvals: .required_pull_request_reviews.required_approving_review_count, enforce_admins: .enforce_admins.enabled, force_push_allowed: .allow_force_pushes.enabled, deletion_allowed: .allow_deletions.enabled}'

# Tooling setup - what is installed, what installs later, and what triggers it

Status: Current

An agent opening this repo on the founding box starts with everything below in the
"ready now" state. Items in "installs later" have explicit triggers - install them WHEN
the trigger fires, not before (right-sizing: tooling earns its place like everything
else).

## Ready now (committed in-repo)

| Thing | Where | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Process seed | `CLAUDE.md` | The operating rules, scars attached |
| Constitution | `docs/constitution.md` | RATIFIED 2026-07-21, before any code |
| Healing Loop | `docs/the-healing-loop.md` | The process metabolism |
| `car` agent type | `.claude/agents/car.md` | No-delegation implementer/reviewer (toolset-enforced) |
| `/goodnight` skill | `.claude/skills/goodnight/` | Session-end ritual + resume packets |
| Resume-packet hook | `.claude/hooks/goodnight-resume-check.sh` | SessionStart announce, wired in `.claude/settings.json` |
| Board tooling | `scripts/board.ps1` + `Board.psm1` (+ Pester tests) | Defaults: user project 6, repo polecatspeaks/StarCar; pure logic Pester-tested |
| Templates | `docs/templates/` | Car brief, state ledger, gating matrix |
| MCP: context7 | `.mcp.json` | Library docs lookup, stack-agnostic |
| MCP: gitnexus | `.mcp.json` | Server registered; serves nothing until first index (below) |
| pr-review-toolkit plugin | `.claude/settings.json` | Enabled |

Also available from the box/user level (nothing to install per-repo): the superpowers
plugin (brainstorming, writing-plans, TDD skills), the Claude-in-Chrome browser tools.

## Installs later - trigger-gated

| Thing | Trigger | How |
|---|---|---|
| GitNexus index | FIRST CODE lands (nothing to index before that) | `npx gitnexus analyze` (or `npm i -g gitnexus` then `gitnexus analyze`); rerun with `--pdg` once the codebase is worth statement-level analysis. The MCP server in `.mcp.json` starts serving as soon as the index exists. |
| CI + repo policy | First workflow need (likely: first code PR) | Port the docs `Status:` line checker + a build/test workflow; keep the "verified means CI went green" rule from CLAUDE.md in force from day one |
| Session-start env hook | The repo is opened in a web/container environment | Port the ancestor's `session-start.sh` PATTERN (idempotent installs, strictly non-fatal guards, wrapper-on-PATH) with THIS stack's tools - the ancestor's dotnet/pwsh specifics do not apply |
| Suite runner / CI watcher scripts | First test suite / first CI | Generalize from the ancestor shop's `run-suites` / `watch-ci` patterns when there is something to run |

## Entire.io session mirroring: ENABLED (owner decision, 2026-07-21)

Every agent-session transcript in this repo publishes to the public checkpoint branch -
by design ("the full monty"): the development process is the showcase, radically
transparent and verifiably real, half-thoughts included. The price of that is the HARD
INVARIANT at the top of CLAUDE.md: StarCar sessions are StarCar-only; nothing from any
other project is ever typed here. `entire enable` + `entire agent add claude-code` are
done (hooks in `.claude/settings.json`, config in `.entire/`); `entire search` is the
entry point for decision archaeology.

lines: | MCP: context7 | `.mcp.json` | Library docs lookup, stack-agnostic |
| MCP: gitnexus | `.mcp.json` | Server registered; serves nothing until first index (below) |
| pr-review-toolkit plugin | `.claude/settings.json` | Enabled |

+| Branch protection on `main` | GitHub repo settings (remote) | Require PR, 0 approvals, no force-push, no deletion, admins exempt. The mechanical half of the STANDING ORDER in `CLAUDE.md`; admins stay exempt so the owner can always override (Law 2). |
+| `dev` integration branch | remote + local | Work integrates here; car branches cut from and merge back to `dev`; `dev` reaches `main` by PR only |
Also available from the box/user level (nothing to install per-repo): the superpowers
plugin (brainstorming, writing-plans, TDD skills), the Claude-in-Chrome browser tools.

Two guards are parked here specifically and should land with it: an area-label presence check (#3) and a check that PRs touching `docs/` were reviewed (#4) - both are prose-only today. The repo-policy half of this row shipped early (see branch protection above); its trigger fired on founding day when the STANDING ORDER was issued. |

repo policy" row on founding day, so it moves to "ready now" with its live
configuration recorded (require PR, 0 approvals, admins exempt - GitHub
forbids self-approval, so requiring one would have frozen main on a
solo-owner repo, and Law 2 says the dispatcher can always override).

The CI half stays parked and now names the two guards waiting on it: the
area-label check (#3) and the docs-were-reviewed check (#4). Both are prose
today. Writing down which rules are unenforced is the point - the Healing
Loop calls the gap between what is written and what is enforced its own
failure surface, and an undocumented gap is one nobody closes.

Refs #4

aiTitle: Review StarCar repo documentation and first issue

content.text:
`dev` pushed, and Entire mirrored the checkpoint branch on the way out — the public transcript pipeline is confirmed live, not just configured.

State of the order: **written** (CLAUDE.md, scar attached), **enforced** (branch protection, verified live), **tracked** (#4, on the board), and **the residual gap is documented rather than assumed closed** (nothing checks that a docs PR was reviewed until CI exists).

But the guard has been *configured* and never *observed to fire*, and this repo's own doctrine is unusually pointed about that — regression-vault tests that pass on arrival prove nothing until you fault-inject once and watch the failure. Three things to settle:

questions.question: Fault-inject the branch protection to prove it actually fires? CLAUDE.md's own rule for guards that pass on arrival is to inject the fault once, observe the failure, revert, and document it.

options.label: Yes, inject it (Recommended)
options.description: I make a throwaway commit on a temp branch and try to push it straight to main. Expected: GitHub rejects it, I capture the exact refusal text into the issue as evidence, and literally nothing changed. If it unexpectedly SUCCEEDS we have learned the guard is fake while it is cheap to learn - and cleanup is guaranteed possible, because a push succeeding proves pushes work.
options.preview:
git push origin temp:main

expected: ! [remote rejected] main (protected branch)
residue on success-path: NONE

if it wrongly succeeds:
  -> guard is fake, we know today
  -> revert is pushable (pushes evidently work)
  -> residue: 2 junk commits, public history

options.label: No, the API read-back is enough
options.description: The live config was read back from GitHub and shows require_pr true, force-push and deletion blocked. GitHub's enforcement of that config is not something this project needs to re-verify. Cost: the guard remains one whose firing nobody has ever seen, which is the exact shape the repo distrusts elsewhere.
options.preview:
evidence we have:
  require_pr        : true
  force_push        : blocked
  deletion          : blocked

evidence we lack:
  anyone ever seeing it refuse a push

options.label: Each document gets a branch and a PR into dev (Recommended)
options.description: The design doc, the spec, and the plan each land on their own branch, opened as a PR into dev, with the adversarial reviewer's verdict posted AS the PR review - APPROVE or REJECT, findings inline on the diff. This makes the README's promise literal: review verdicts committed in-repo as they happen, in GitHub's native review surface, publicly readable. A REJECT becomes a visible artifact instead of a paragraph in a chat log.

verdicts become public artifacts

content.text:
**The fault injection caught a real failure. The push succeeded.**

```
remote: Bypassed rule violations for refs/heads/main:
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
   1729eb7..b6443c1  tmp/protection-probe -> main
=== EXIT CODE: 0 ===
```

`main` now carries `b6443c1` and a `.protection-probe` file. GitHub didn't refuse — it logged the violation and **let it through**.

**Root cause:** I authenticate as `polecatspeaks`, whose permission on this repo is `admin: true`. We set `enforce_admins: false`. So the rule was evaluated, matched, and bypassed on identity.

**The class, not the instance:** *an exemption keyed to identity protects nothing when every actor shares that identity.* Agents in this repo act **with your credential**. There is no technical distinction between "the owner pushing" and "an agent pushing" — they are the same token. So "admins exempt" and "nobody is bound" are the same sentence, and the guard was decorative from the moment it was applied.

**That recommendation was mine, and the preview I wrote said `agents: cannot reach main, period`. That was false.** I derived it from Law 2 — the dispatcher outranks the board, the system never resists an override — but I misapplied the law by conflating your *authority* with your *credential*. Blocking the agent from `main` was never resisting you; your override path is the settings page, and it stays yours either way. The law argued for keeping you able to override, not for keeping the token able to.

The good news is the honest one: this cost ~90 seconds and one junk commit, on founding day, before anything depended on it. Had we trusted the API read-back — which showed `require_pr: true` and looked perfect — the first time we'd have learned it was that a car pushed to `main` mid-train.

questions.question: How do we make the guard actually bind? The constraint we now know: agents authenticate as you, with admin, so any identity-based exemption exempts everyone.

options.label: enforce_admins: true (Recommended)
options.description: The rule binds every actor including your own pushes. Your override becomes a deliberate, visible act - toggle protection off in settings, do the thing, toggle it back - rather than something a reflexive git push can do silently. This is the only setting that enforces the order given one shared credential, and it honors Law 2 properly: your authority is intact, your token is not privileged.
options.preview:
enforce_admins: true

agent push to main : REFUSED (really)
owner push to main : REFUSED
owner override     : settings toggle, deliberate

one credential -> one rule, no exemption to abuse

options.label: enforce_admins: true AND plan a separate agent identity
options.description: Same immediate fix, plus we open a ticket for the real structural repair: give agents their own credential (machine account or fine-grained token with push but not admin) so the exemption becomes meaningful again and you regain a genuine personal override. Costs setup and token management, and it is the honest long-term answer to a problem we only just discovered we have.
options.preview:
now:   enforce_admins = true
later: agents get their OWN token
         push: yes, admin: no
       owner keeps a real, personal override

fixes the CLASS, not just this instance
cost: token setup + rotation burden

options.label: Leave it exempt, rely on the written rule
options.description: Revert to trusting CLAUDE.md and my compliance. Cost: we would be knowingly keeping a guard that we have now WATCHED fail - which is worse than having no guard, because the setup doc and the issue both claim main is protected. A lying instrument poisons everything downstream of it.
options.preview:
protection: present but bypassable
docs claim: main is protected
reality   : it is not

-> the lying-canary shape, deliberately kept

questions.question: How do we clean the probe commit off main? It is currently public.

options.label: Revert by PR (Recommended)
options.description: Open the repo's first pull request: a branch that reverts b6443c1, PR'd into main, merged. It dogfoods the corrected protection immediately, produces a real PR as the first thing to land under the standing order, and leaves the incident visible in history - which is this repo's entire thesis. The probe commit becomes a permanent, honest artifact of the founding-day failure rather than something quietly erased.

  PR -> main  (first PR in the repo)
  merge

history keeps: probe commit + its revert
proves: corrected guard works, via real use
ethos : scars stay attached

options.label: Force-push main back to 1729eb7
options.description: Erases the probe commit entirely; main looks as though it never happened. Cleaner-looking public history. Cost: it requires an admin bypass to do (the same bypass we just condemned), it rewrites public history on a repo whose thesis is that its history is an honest record, and it destroys the evidence of the one incident most worth showing.

input.command:
cd /c/Users/Chris/git/starcar
gh pr create --repo polecatspeaks/StarCar --base main --head fix/revert-protection-probe \
  --title "Revert the branch-protection probe commit from main" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Reverts `b6443c1`, which reached `main` by direct push on founding day.

## What happened

A STANDING ORDER was issued (never commit to `main`; everything lands by PR, documentation included). Branch protection was applied as the mechanical guard, and the live config read back exactly as intended: `require_pr: true`, force-push and deletion blocked, `enforce_admins: false`.

Per `CLAUDE.md`'s rule that a guard which passes on arrival proves nothing until you fault-inject it once and observe the failure, a deliberate violation was attempted: a throwaway commit pushed straight to `main`.

**It succeeded.**

```
remote: Bypassed rule violations for refs/heads/main:
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
   1729eb7..b6443c1  tmp/protection-probe -> main
exit code: 0
```

## Root cause, classified

The agent authenticates as `polecatspeaks`, whose repo permission is `admin: true`. With `enforce_admins: false`, the rule was evaluated, matched, and bypassed on identity.

**The class:** *an exemption keyed to identity protects nothing when every actor shares that identity.* Agents here act with the owner's credential - there is no technical distinction between "the owner pushing" and "an agent pushing", because it is one token. `admins exempt` and `nobody is bound` were the same sentence.

The reasoning error underneath it: Law 2 (the dispatcher commands, the system never resists an override) was read as an argument for exempting the owner's *credential*, when it is an argument for preserving the owner's *authority*. The override path is the settings page, and it remains the owner's under either configuration. Authority and credential are not the same thing, and conflating them produced a guard that bound nobody.

## Fix

`enforce_admins: true`. The same fault was then re-injected against the corrected config:

```
remote: error: GH006: Protected branch update failed for refs/heads/main.
remote: - Changes must be made through a pull request.
 ! [remote rejected] tmp/protection-probe -> main (protected branch hook declined)
exit code: 1
```

Before-and-after on identical input. The guard is now non-vacuous, observed rather than asserted.

## Why the probe commit is reverted rather than erased

Force-pushing `main` back would have hidden the incident, required the very admin bypass being condemned, and rewritten public history in a repo whose thesis is that its history is an honest record. The probe commit and this revert both stay. The scar is the artifact.

This PR is also the first exercise of the corrected rule - it is here because a direct push is no longer possible.

Refs #4
EOF
)"

file.content:
**Right-sizing rule (read first):** the full ladder below is calibrated for consequential,
multi-car work. Scale ceremony to stakes: a small, coverage-class change takes a car and
one adversarial reviewer; only structural work (new subsystems, rewrites, cross-boundary
contracts) pays the full design → spec → plan ladder. Porting maximum ceremony onto every
change is itself a process failure (the autoimmune mode - see the Healing Loop's edges).

## Test-Driven Development (non-negotiable)

Every behavior change is developed red-first: write the failing test, RUN it, confirm it
fails for the stated reason, then write the minimum code to green, then refactor.

- A test that passes the moment you write it proves nothing.
- Bugs start with a reproducing test; the fix is done when it goes green.
- Regression-vault tests that pass on arrival prove non-vacuity instead: fault-inject the
  guarded behavior once, observe the failure, revert, document.
- State the pass counts before every commit. Never commit unbuilt or untested work.

*Scar: every clean landing this seed descends from was red-first; every incident retro

input.old_string: ## Test-Driven Development (non-negotiable)
input.new_string:
## NORTH STAR: documentation ranks equal to code

Not "documented code." Documentation and code are two halves of one deliverable, held to
one standard. Designs, specs, plans, review verdicts, ledgers, briefs, agent definitions,
and these rules themselves ARE the product - in a shop where the workforce evaporates
nightly, the written system is the only system that survives, so a defect in the writing
is a defect in the product, not a cosmetic issue. Every rule below that binds code binds
documents: review, gates, PRs, honest stops, REJECT authority.

**Documents are living, never static.** A document is true only at the moment of its
commit. The instant the code diverges from it, that document has become a lying canary -
a reader trusting a described mechanism the code no longer implements - and this project
holds that a confident falsehood on an information surface is worse than a blank one.
Therefore: **the commit that invalidates a document updates that document, in the same
commit.** Not a follow-up ticket, not a cleanup pass, not "we will true it up before the
release." The state-ledger rule below is this principle applied to one file; it
generalizes to every file.

**Meat and bones first; pretty later.** Correctness, completeness, and citation truth are
the expensive parts and come first. Formatting, prose polish, and structure-tidying are
cheap and can happen any time afterward. Never let a polish pass be mistaken for
documentation work, and never let unpolished prose delay a document that is correct.

*Scar: this order was issued on founding day after the session had committed five times
to main and stood one approval away from committing the founding design document - the
document every later contract would have inherited as ground truth - with no gate over
any of it, on the theory that prose is cheaper than code. In a project whose deliverables
are mostly documents, exempting prose from review exempts most of the product.*

## Test-Driven Development (non-negotiable)

credential, so any admin exemption is an exemption for every agent too. The owner's
override is the settings page, which no configuration takes away. Authority and
credential are different things, and a guard that confuses them protects nothing.

*Scar: paid the same hour the order was issued. Protection was applied with
`enforce_admins: false` so the owner could always override, and the live config read back
perfect - `require_pr: true`, force-push and deletion blocked. The mandated fault
injection then pushed straight to `main` and GitHub answered `Bypassed rule violations`
with exit code 0. The guard had been decorative from the moment it was applied, and the
setup doc and its issue both already claimed `main` was protected. Re-injected against
`enforce_admins: true`: `GH006: Protected branch update failed`, exit 1. Cost: one junk
commit on a public `main`, reverted through the repo's first PR (#5), roughly ninety
seconds. Had the API read-back been trusted, the discovery would instead have been a car
pushing to `main` mid-train.*

before the design landed, which is the only reason it cost nothing.*
